33	if os.name == "nt":
34	    BEFORE_BAR = "\r"

41	class ProgressBar(t.Generic[V]):
42	    def __init__(
43	        self,
44	        iterable: cabc.Iterable[V] | None,
45	        length: int | None = None,
46	        fill_char: str = "#",
47	        empty_char: str = " ",
48	        bar_template: str = "%(bar)s",
49	        info_sep: str = "  ",
50	        hidden: bool = False,
51	        show_eta: bool = True,
52	        show_percent: bool | None = None,
53	        show_pos: bool = False,
54	        item_show_func: t.Callable[[V | None], str | None] | None = None,
55	        label: str | None = None,
56	        file: t.TextIO | None = None,
57	        color: bool | None = None,
58	        update_min_steps: int = 1,
59	        width: int = 30,
60	    ) -> None:
61	        self.fill_char = fill_char
62	        self.empty_char = empty_char
63	        self.bar_template = bar_template
64	        self.info_sep = info_sep
65	        self.hidden = hidden
66	        self.show_eta = show_eta
67	        self.show_percent = show_percent
68	        self.show_pos = show_pos
69	        self.item_show_func = item_show_func
70	        self.label: str = label or ""
71	
72	        if file is None:
73	            file = _default_text_stdout()
74	
75	            # There are no standard streams attached to write to. For example,
76	            # pythonw on Windows.
77	            if file is None:
78	                file = StringIO()
79	
80	        self.file = file
81	        self.color = color
82	        self.update_min_steps = update_min_steps
83	        self._completed_intervals = 0
84	        self.width: int = width
85	        self.autowidth: bool = width == 0
86	
87	        if length is None:
88	            from operator import length_hint
89	
90	            length = length_hint(iterable, -1)
91	
92	            if length == -1:
93	                length = None
94	        if iterable is None:
95	            if length is None:
96	                raise TypeError("iterable or length is required")
97	            iterable = t.cast("cabc.Iterable[V]", range(length))
98	        self.iter: cabc.Iterable[V] = iter(iterable)
99	        self.length = length
100	        self.pos = 0
101	        self.avg: list[float] = []
102	        self.last_eta: float
103	        self.start: float
104	        self.start = self.last_eta = time.time()
105	        self.eta_known: bool = False
106	        self.finished: bool = False
107	        self.max_width: int | None = None
108	        self.entered: bool = False
109	        self.current_item: V | None = None
110	        self._is_atty = isatty(self.file)
111	        self._last_line: str | None = None
112	
113	    def __enter__(self) -> ProgressBar[V]:
114	        self.entered = True
115	        self.render_progress()
116	        return self
117	
118	    def __exit__(
119	        self,
120	        exc_type: type[BaseException] | None,
121	        exc_value: BaseException | None,
122	        tb: TracebackType | None,
123	    ) -> None:
124	        self.render_finish()
125	
126	    def __iter__(self) -> cabc.Iterator[V]:
127	        if not self.entered:
128	            raise RuntimeError("You need to use progress bars in a with block.")
129	        self.render_progress()
130	        return self.generator()
131	
132	    def __next__(self) -> V:
133	        # Iteration is defined in terms of a generator function,
134	        # returned by iter(self); use that to define next(). This works
135	        # because `self.iter` is an iterable consumed by that generator,
136	        # so it is re-entry safe. Calling `next(self.generator())`
137	        # twice works and does "what you want".
138	        return next(iter(self))
139	
140	    def render_finish(self) -> None:
141	        if self.hidden or not self._is_atty:
142	            return
143	        self.file.write(AFTER_BAR)
144	        self.file.flush()
145	
146	    @property
147	    def pct(self) -> float:
148	        if self.finished:
149	            return 1.0
150	        return min(self.pos / (float(self.length or 1) or 1), 1.0)
151	
152	    @property
153	    def time_per_iteration(self) -> float:
154	        if not self.avg:
155	            return 0.0
156	        return sum(self.avg) / float(len(self.avg))
157	
158	    @property
159	    def eta(self) -> float:
160	        if self.length is not None and not self.finished:
161	            return self.time_per_iteration * (self.length - self.pos)
162	        return 0.0
163	
164	    def format_eta(self) -> str:
165	        if self.eta_known:
166	            t = int(self.eta)
167	            seconds = t % 60
168	            t //= 60
169	            minutes = t % 60
170	            t //= 60
171	            hours = t % 24
172	            t //= 24
173	            if t > 0:
174	                return f"{t}d {hours:02}:{minutes:02}:{seconds:02}"
175	            else:
176	                return f"{hours:02}:{minutes:02}:{seconds:02}"
177	        return ""
178	
179	    def format_pos(self) -> str:
180	        pos = str(self.pos)
181	        if self.length is not None:
182	            pos += f"/{self.length}"
183	        return pos
184	
185	    def format_pct(self) -> str:
186	        return f"{int(self.pct * 100): 4}%"[1:]
187	
188	    def format_bar(self) -> str:
189	        if self.length is not None:
190	            bar_length = int(self.pct * self.width)
191	            bar = self.fill_char * bar_length
192	            bar += self.empty_char * (self.width - bar_length)
193	        elif self.finished:
194	            bar = self.fill_char * self.width
195	        else:
196	            chars = list(self.empty_char * (self.width or 1))
197	            if self.time_per_iteration != 0:
198	                chars[
199	                    int(
200	                        (math.cos(self.pos * self.time_per_iteration) / 2.0 + 0.5)
201	                        * self.width
202	                    )
203	                ] = self.fill_char
204	            bar = "".join(chars)
205	        return bar
206	
207	    def format_progress_line(self) -> str:
208	        show_percent = self.show_percent
209	
210	        info_bits = []
211	        if self.length is not None and show_percent is None:
212	            show_percent = not self.show_pos
213	
214	        if self.show_pos:
215	            info_bits.append(self.format_pos())
216	        if show_percent:
217	            info_bits.append(self.format_pct())
218	        if self.show_eta and self.eta_known and not self.finished:
219	            info_bits.append(self.format_eta())
220	        if self.item_show_func is not None:
221	            item_info = self.item_show_func(self.current_item)
222	            if item_info is not None:
223	                info_bits.append(item_info)
224	
225	        return (
226	            self.bar_template
227	            % {
228	                "label": self.label,
229	                "bar": self.format_bar(),
230	                "info": self.info_sep.join(info_bits),
231	            }
232	        ).rstrip()
233	
234	    def render_progress(self) -> None:
235	        import shutil
236	
237	        if self.hidden:
238	            return
239	
240	        if not self._is_atty:
241	            # Only output the label once if the output is not a TTY.
242	            if self._last_line != self.label:
243	                self._last_line = self.label
244	                echo(self.label, file=self.file, color=self.color)
245	            return
246	
247	        buf = []
248	        # Update width in case the terminal has been resized
249	        if self.autowidth:
250	            old_width = self.width
251	            self.width = 0
252	            clutter_length = term_len(self.format_progress_line())
253	            new_width = max(0, shutil.get_terminal_size().columns - clutter_length)
254	            if new_width < old_width:
255	                buf.append(BEFORE_BAR)
256	                buf.append(" " * self.max_width)  # type: ignore
257	                self.max_width = new_width
258	            self.width = new_width
259	
260	        clear_width = self.width
261	        if self.max_width is not None:
262	            clear_width = self.max_width
263	
264	        buf.append(BEFORE_BAR)
265	        line = self.format_progress_line()
266	        line_len = term_len(line)
267	        if self.max_width is None or self.max_width < line_len:
268	            self.max_width = line_len
269	
270	        buf.append(line)
271	        buf.append(" " * (clear_width - line_len))
272	        line = "".join(buf)
273	        # Render the line only if it changed.
274	
275	        if line != self._last_line:
276	            self._last_line = line
277	            echo(line, file=self.file, color=self.color, nl=False)
278	            self.file.flush()
279	
280	    def make_step(self, n_steps: int) -> None:
281	        self.pos += n_steps
282	        if self.length is not None and self.pos >= self.length:
283	            self.finished = True
284	
285	        if (time.time() - self.last_eta) < 1.0:
286	            return
287	
288	        self.last_eta = time.time()
289	
290	        # self.avg is a rolling list of length <= 7 of steps where steps are
291	        # defined as time elapsed divided by the total progress through
292	        # self.length.
293	        if self.pos:
294	            step = (time.time() - self.start) / self.pos
295	        else:
296	            step = time.time() - self.start
297	
298	        self.avg = self.avg[-6:] + [step]
299	
300	        self.eta_known = self.length is not None
301	
302	    def update(self, n_steps: int, current_item: V | None = None) -> None:
303	        """Update the progress bar by advancing a specified number of
304	        steps, and optionally set the ``current_item`` for this new
305	        position.
306	
307	        :param n_steps: Number of steps to advance.
308	        :param current_item: Optional item to set as ``current_item``
309	            for the updated position.
310	
311	        .. versionchanged:: 8.0
312	            Added the ``current_item`` optional parameter.
313	
314	        .. versionchanged:: 8.0
315	            Only render when the number of steps meets the
316	            ``update_min_steps`` threshold.
317	        """
318	        if current_item is not None:
319	            self.current_item = current_item
320	
321	        self._completed_intervals += n_steps
322	
323	        if self._completed_intervals >= self.update_min_steps:
324	            self.make_step(self._completed_intervals)
325	            self.render_progress()
326	            self._completed_intervals = 0
327	
328	    def finish(self) -> None:
329	        self.eta_known = False
330	        self.current_item = None
331	        self.finished = True
332	
333	    def generator(self) -> cabc.Iterator[V]:
334	        """Return a generator which yields the items added to the bar
335	        during construction, and updates the progress bar *after* the
336	        yielded block returns.
337	        """
338	        # WARNING: the iterator interface for `ProgressBar` relies on
339	        # this and only works because this is a simple generator which
340	        # doesn't create or manage additional state. If this function
341	        # changes, the impact should be evaluated both against
342	        # `iter(bar)` and `next(bar)`. `next()` in particular may call
343	        # `self.generator()` repeatedly, and this must remain safe in
344	        # order for that interface to work.
345	        if not self.entered:
346	            raise RuntimeError("You need to use progress bars in a with block.")
347	
348	        if not self._is_atty:
349	            yield from self.iter
350	        else:
351	            for rv in self.iter:
352	                self.current_item = rv
353	
354	                # This allows show_item_func to be updated before the
355	                # item is processed. Only trigger at the beginning of
356	                # the update interval.
357	                if self._completed_intervals == 0:
358	                    self.render_progress()
359	
360	                yield rv
361	                self.update(1)
362	
363	            self.finish()
364	            self.render_progress()

367	def pager(generator: cabc.Iterable[str], color: bool | None = None) -> None:
368	    """Decide what method to use for paging through text."""
369	    stdout = _default_text_stdout()
370	
371	    # There are no standard streams attached to write to. For example,
372	    # pythonw on Windows.
373	    if stdout is None:
374	        stdout = StringIO()
375	
376	    if not isatty(sys.stdin) or not isatty(stdout):
377	        return _nullpager(stdout, generator, color)
378	    pager_cmd = (os.environ.get("PAGER", None) or "").strip()
379	    if pager_cmd:
380	        if WIN:
381	            return _tempfilepager(generator, pager_cmd, color)
382	        return _pipepager(generator, pager_cmd, color)
383	    if os.environ.get("TERM") in ("dumb", "emacs"):
384	        return _nullpager(stdout, generator, color)
385	    if WIN or sys.platform.startswith("os2"):
386	        return _tempfilepager(generator, "more <", color)
387	    if hasattr(os, "system") and os.system("(less) 2>/dev/null") == 0:
388	        return _pipepager(generator, "less", color)
389	
390	    import tempfile
391	
392	    fd, filename = tempfile.mkstemp()
393	    os.close(fd)
394	    try:
395	        if hasattr(os, "system") and os.system(f'more "{filename}"') == 0:
396	            return _pipepager(generator, "more", color)
397	        return _nullpager(stdout, generator, color)
398	    finally:
399	        os.unlink(filename)
400

402	def _pipepager(generator: cabc.Iterable[str], cmd: str, color: bool | None) -> None:
403	    """Page through text by feeding it to another program.  Invoking a
404	    pager through this might support colors.
405	    """
406	    import subprocess
407	
408	    env = dict(os.environ)
409	
410	    # If we're piping to less we might support colors under the
411	    # condition that
412	    cmd_detail = cmd.rsplit("/", 1)[-1].split()
413	    if color is None and cmd_detail[0] == "less":
414	        less_flags = f"{os.environ.get('LESS', '')}{' '.join(cmd_detail[1:])}"
415	        if not less_flags:
416	            env["LESS"] = "-R"
417	            color = True
418	        elif "r" in less_flags or "R" in less_flags:
419	            color = True
420	
421	    c = subprocess.Popen(cmd, shell=True, stdin=subprocess.PIPE, env=env)
422	    stdin = t.cast(t.BinaryIO, c.stdin)
423	    encoding = get_best_encoding(stdin)
424	    try:
425	        for text in generator:
426	            if not color:
427	                text = strip_ansi(text)
428	
429	            stdin.write(text.encode(encoding, "replace"))
430	    except (OSError, KeyboardInterrupt):
431	        pass
432	    else:
433	        stdin.close()
434	
435	    # Less doesn't respect ^C, but catches it for its own UI purposes (aborting
436	    # search or other commands inside less).
437	    #
438	    # That means when the user hits ^C, the parent process (click) terminates,
439	    # but less is still alive, paging the output and messing up the terminal.
440	    #
441	    # If the user wants to make the pager exit on ^C, they should set
442	    # `LESS='-K'`. It's not our decision to make.
443	    while True:
444	        try:
445	            c.wait()
446	        except KeyboardInterrupt:
447	            pass
448	        else:
449	            break
450	
451	
452	def _tempfilepager(generator: cabc.Iterable[str], cmd: str, color: bool | None) -> None:
453	    """Page through text by invoking a program on a temporary file."""
454	    import tempfile
455	
456	    fd, filename = tempfile.mkstemp()
457	    # TODO: This never terminates if the passed generator never terminates.
458	    text = "".join(generator)
459	    if not color:
460	        text = strip_ansi(text)
461	    encoding = get_best_encoding(sys.stdout)
462	    with open_stream(filename, "wb")[0] as f:
463	        f.write(text.encode(encoding))
464	    try:
465	        os.system(f'{cmd} "{filename}"')
466	    finally:
467	        os.close(fd)
468	        os.unlink(filename)

471	def _nullpager(
472	    stream: t.TextIO, generator: cabc.Iterable[str], color: bool | None
473	) -> None:
474	    """Simply print unformatted text.  This is the ultimate fallback."""
475	    for text in generator:
476	        if not color:
477	            text = strip_ansi(text)
478	        stream.write(text)

481	class Editor:
482	    def __init__(
483	        self,
484	        editor: str | None = None,
485	        env: cabc.Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
486	        require_save: bool = True,
487	        extension: str = ".txt",
488	    ) -> None:
489	        self.editor = editor
490	        self.env = env
491	        self.require_save = require_save
492	        self.extension = extension
493	
494	    def get_editor(self) -> str:
495	        if self.editor is not None:
496	            return self.editor
497	        for key in "VISUAL", "EDITOR":
498	            rv = os.environ.get(key)
499	            if rv:
500	                return rv
501	        if WIN:
502	            return "notepad"
503	        for editor in "sensible-editor", "vim", "nano":
504	            if os.system(f"which {editor} >/dev/null 2>&1") == 0:
505	                return editor
506	        return "vi"
507	
508	    def edit_file(self, filename: str) -> None:
509	        import subprocess
510	
511	        editor = self.get_editor()
512	        environ: dict[str, str] | None = None
513	
514	        if self.env:
515	            environ = os.environ.copy()
516	            environ.update(self.env)
517	
518	        try:
519	            c = subprocess.Popen(f'{editor} "{filename}"', env=environ, shell=True)
520	            exit_code = c.wait()
521	            if exit_code != 0:
522	                raise ClickException(
523	                    _("{editor}: Editing failed").format(editor=editor)
524	                )
525	        except OSError as e:
526	            raise ClickException(
527	                _("{editor}: Editing failed: {e}").format(editor=editor, e=e)
528	            ) from e
529	
530	    def edit(self, text: t.AnyStr | None) -> t.AnyStr | None:
531	        import tempfile
532	
533	        if not text:
534	            data = b""
535	        elif isinstance(text, (bytes, bytearray)):
536	            data = text
537	        else:
538	            if text and not text.endswith("\n"):
539	                text += "\n"
540	
541	            if WIN:
542	                data = text.replace("\n", "\r\n").encode("utf-8-sig")
543	            else:
544	                data = text.encode("utf-8")
545	
546	        fd, name = tempfile.mkstemp(prefix="editor-", suffix=self.extension)
547	        f: t.BinaryIO
548	
549	        try:
550	            with os.fdopen(fd, "wb") as f:
551	                f.write(data)
552	
553	            # If the filesystem resolution is 1 second, like Mac OS
554	            # 10.12 Extended, or 2 seconds, like FAT32, and the editor
555	            # closes very fast, require_save can fail. Set the modified
556	            # time to be 2 seconds in the past to work around this.
557	            os.utime(name, (os.path.getatime(name), os.path.getmtime(name) - 2))
558	            # Depending on the resolution, the exact value might not be
559	            # recorded, so get the new recorded value.
560	            timestamp = os.path.getmtime(name)
561	
562	            self.edit_file(name)
563	
564	            if self.require_save and os.path.getmtime(name) == timestamp:
565	                return None
566	
567	            with open(name, "rb") as f:
568	                rv = f.read()
569	
570	            if isinstance(text, (bytes, bytearray)):
571	                return rv
572	
573	            return rv.decode("utf-8-sig").replace("\r\n", "\n")  # type: ignore
574	        finally:
575	            os.unlink(name)

578	def open_url(url: str, wait: bool = False, locate: bool = False) -> int:
579	    import subprocess
580	
581	    def _unquote_file(url: str) -> str:
582	        from urllib.parse import unquote
583	
584	        if url.startswith("file://"):
585	            url = unquote(url[7:])
586	
587	        return url
588	
589	    if sys.platform == "darwin":
590	        args = ["open"]
591	        if wait:
592	            args.append("-W")
593	        if locate:
594	            args.append("-R")
595	        args.append(_unquote_file(url))
596	        null = open("/dev/null", "w")
597	        try:
598	            return subprocess.Popen(args, stderr=null).wait()
599	        finally:
600	            null.close()
601	    elif WIN:
602	        if locate:
603	            url = _unquote_file(url.replace('"', ""))
604	            args = f'explorer /select,"{url}"'
605	        else:
606	            url = url.replace('"', "")
607	            wait_str = "/WAIT" if wait else ""
608	            args = f'start {wait_str} "" "{url}"'
609	        return os.system(args)
610	    elif CYGWIN:
611	        if locate:
612	            url = os.path.dirname(_unquote_file(url).replace('"', ""))
613	            args = f'cygstart "{url}"'
614	        else:
615	            url = url.replace('"', "")
616	            wait_str = "-w" if wait else ""
617	            args = f'cygstart {wait_str} "{url}"'
618	        return os.system(args)
619	
620	    try:
621	        if locate:

638	def _translate_ch_to_exc(ch: str) -> None:
639	    if ch == "\x03":
640	        raise KeyboardInterrupt()
641	
642	    if ch == "\x04" and not WIN:  # Unix-like, Ctrl+D
643	        raise EOFError()
644	
645	    if ch == "\x1a" and WIN:  # Windows, Ctrl+Z
646	        raise EOFError()
647	
648	    return None
649	
650	
651	if WIN:
652	    import msvcrt
653	
654	    @contextlib.contextmanager
655	    def raw_terminal() -> cabc.Iterator[int]:
656	        yield -1
657	
658	    def getchar(echo: bool) -> str:
659	        # The function `getch` will return a bytes object corresponding to
660	        # the pressed character. Since Windows 10 build 1803, it will also
661	        # return \x00 when called a second time after pressing a regular key.
662	        #
663	        # `getwch` does not share this probably-bugged behavior. Moreover, it
664	        # returns a Unicode object by default, which is what we want.
665	        #
666	        # Either of these functions will return \x00 or \xe0 to indicate
667	        # a special key, and you need to call the same function again to get
668	        # the "rest" of the code. The fun part is that \u00e0 is
669	        # "latin small letter a with grave", so if you type that on a French
670	        # keyboard, you _also_ get a \xe0.
671	        # E.g., consider the Up arrow. This returns \xe0 and then \x48. The
672	        # resulting Unicode string reads as "a with grave" + "capital H".
673	        # This is indistinguishable from when the user actually types
674	        # "a with grave" and then "capital H".
675	        #
676	        # When \xe0 is returned, we assume it's part of a special-key sequence
677	        # and call `getwch` again, but that means that when the user types
678	        # the \u00e0 character, `getchar` doesn't return until a second
679	        # character is typed.
680	        # The alternative is returning immediately, but that would mess up
681	        # cross-platform handling of arrow keys and others that start with
682	        # \xe0. Another option is using `getch`, but then we can't reliably
683	        # read non-ASCII characters, because return values of `getch` are
684	        # limited to the current 8-bit codepage.
685	        #
686	        # Anyway, Click doesn't claim to do this Right(tm), and using `getwch`
687	        # is doing the right thing in more situations than with `getch`.
688	        func: t.Callable[[], str]
689	
690	        if echo:
691	            func = msvcrt.getwche  # type: ignore
692	        else:
693	            func = msvcrt.getwch  # type: ignore
694	
695	        rv = func()
696	
697	        if rv in ("\x00", "\xe0"):
698	            # \x00 and \xe0 are control characters that indicate special key,
699	            # see above.
700	            rv += func()
701	
702	        _translate_ch_to_exc(rv)
703	        return rv
704	
705	else:
706	    import termios
707	    import tty
708	
709	    @contextlib.contextmanager
710	    def raw_terminal() -> cabc.Iterator[int]:
711	        f: t.TextIO | None
712	        fd: int
713	
714	        if not isatty(sys.stdin):
715	            f = open("/dev/tty")
716	            fd = f.fileno()
717	        else:
718	            fd = sys.stdin.fileno()
719	            f = None
720	
721	        try:
722	            old_settings = termios.tcgetattr(fd)
723	
724	            try:
725	                tty.setraw(fd)
726	                yield fd
727	            finally:
728	                termios.tcsetattr(fd, termios.TCSADRAIN, old_settings)
729	                sys.stdout.flush()
730	
731	                if f is not None:
732	                    f.close()
733	        except termios.error:
734	            pass
735	
736	    def getchar(echo: bool) -> str:
737	        with raw_terminal() as fd:
738	            ch = os.read(fd, 32).decode(get_best_encoding(sys.stdin), "replace")
739	
740	            if echo and isatty(sys.stdout):
741	                sys.stdout.write(ch)
742	
743	            _translate_ch_to_exc(ch)
744	            return ch

83	def prompt(
84	    text: str,
85	    default: t.Any | None = None,
86	    hide_input: bool = False,
87	    confirmation_prompt: bool | str = False,
88	    type: ParamType | t.Any | None = None,
89	    value_proc: t.Callable[[str], t.Any] | None = None,
90	    prompt_suffix: str = ": ",
91	    show_default: bool = True,
92	    err: bool = False,
93	    show_choices: bool = True,
94	) -> t.Any:
95	    """Prompts a user for input.  This is a convenience function that can
96	    be used to prompt a user for input later.
97	
98	    If the user aborts the input by sending an interrupt signal, this
99	    function will catch it and raise a :exc:`Abort` exception.
100	
101	    :param text: the text to show for the prompt.
102	    :param default: the default value to use if no input happens.  If this
103	                    is not given it will prompt until it's aborted.
104	    :param hide_input: if this is set to true then the input value will
105	                       be hidden.
106	    :param confirmation_prompt: Prompt a second time to confirm the
107	        value. Can be set to a string instead of ``True`` to customize
108	        the message.
109	    :param type: the type to use to check the value against.
110	    :param value_proc: if this parameter is provided it's a function that
111	                       is invoked instead of the type conversion to
112	                       convert a value.
113	    :param prompt_suffix: a suffix that should be added to the prompt.
114	    :param show_default: shows or hides the default value in the prompt.
115	    :param err: if set to true the file defaults to ``stderr`` instead of
116	                ``stdout``, the same as with echo.
117	    :param show_choices: Show or hide choices if the passed type is a Choice.
118	                         For example if type is a Choice of either day or week,
119	                         show_choices is true and text is "Group by" then the
120	                         prompt will be "Group by (day, week): ".
121	
122	    .. versionadded:: 8.0
123	        ``confirmation_prompt`` can be a custom string.
124	
125	    .. versionadded:: 7.0
126	        Added the ``show_choices`` parameter.
127	
128	    .. versionadded:: 6.0
129	        Added unicode support for cmd.exe on Windows.
130	
131	    .. versionadded:: 4.0
132	        Added the `err` parameter.
133	
134	    """
135	
136	    def prompt_func(text: str) -> str:
137	        f = hidden_prompt_func if hide_input else visible_prompt_func
138	        try:
139	            # Write the prompt separately so that we get nice
140	            # coloring through colorama on Windows
141	            echo(text.rstrip(" "), nl=False, err=err)
142	            # Echo a space to stdout to work around an issue where
143	            # readline causes backspace to clear the whole line.
144	            return f(" ")
145	        except (KeyboardInterrupt, EOFError):
146	            # getpass doesn't print a newline if the user aborts input with ^C.
147	            # Allegedly this behavior is inherited from getpass(3).
148	            # A doc bug has been filed at https://bugs.python.org/issue24711
149	            if hide_input:
150	                echo(None, err=err)
151	            raise Abort() from None
152	
153	    if value_proc is None:
154	        value_proc = convert_type(type, default)
155	
156	    prompt = _build_prompt(
157	        text, prompt_suffix, show_default, default, show_choices, type
158	    )
159	
160	    if confirmation_prompt:
161	        if confirmation_prompt is True:
162	            confirmation_prompt = _("Repeat for confirmation")
163	
164	        confirmation_prompt = _build_prompt(confirmation_prompt, prompt_suffix)
165	
166	    while True:
167	        while True:
168	            value = prompt_func(prompt)
169	            if value:
170	                break
171	            elif default is not None:
172	                value = default
173	                break
174	        try:
175	            result = value_proc(value)
176	        except UsageError as e:
177	            if hide_input:
178	                echo(_("Error: The value you entered was invalid."), err=err)
179	            else:
180	                echo(_("Error: {e.message}").format(e=e), err=err)
181	            continue
182	        if not confirmation_prompt:
183	            return result
184	        while True:
185	            value2 = prompt_func(confirmation_prompt)
186	            is_empty = not value and not value2
187	            if value2 or is_empty:
188	                break
189	        if value == value2:
190	            return result
191	        echo(_("Error: The two entered values do not match."), err=err)
192

194	def confirm(
195	    text: str,
196	    default: bool | None = False,
197	    abort: bool = False,
198	    prompt_suffix: str = ": ",
199	    show_default: bool = True,
200	    err: bool = False,
201	) -> bool:
202	    """Prompts for confirmation (yes/no question).
203	
204	    If the user aborts the input by sending a interrupt signal this
205	    function will catch it and raise a :exc:`Abort` exception.
206	
207	    :param text: the question to ask.
208	    :param default: The default value to use when no input is given. If
209	        ``None``, repeat until input is given.
210	    :param abort: if this is set to `True` a negative answer aborts the
211	                  exception by raising :exc:`Abort`.
212	    :param prompt_suffix: a suffix that should be added to the prompt.
213	    :param show_default: shows or hides the default value in the prompt.
214	    :param err: if set to true the file defaults to ``stderr`` instead of
215	                ``stdout``, the same as with echo.
216	
217	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
218	        Repeat until input is given if ``default`` is ``None``.
219	
220	    .. versionadded:: 4.0
221	        Added the ``err`` parameter.
222	    """
223	    prompt = _build_prompt(
224	        text,
225	        prompt_suffix,
226	        show_default,
227	        "y/n" if default is None else ("Y/n" if default else "y/N"),
228	    )
229	
230	    while True:
231	        try:
232	            # Write the prompt separately so that we get nice
233	            # coloring through colorama on Windows
234	            echo(prompt.rstrip(" "), nl=False, err=err)
235	            # Echo a space to stdout to work around an issue where
236	            # readline causes backspace to clear the whole line.
237	            value = visible_prompt_func(" ").lower().strip()
238	        except (KeyboardInterrupt, EOFError):
239	            raise Abort() from None
240	        if value in ("y", "yes"):
241	            rv = True
242	        elif value in ("n", "no"):
243	            rv = False
244	        elif default is not None and value == "":
245	            rv = default
246	        else:
247	            echo(_("Error: invalid input"), err=err)
248	            continue
249	        break
250	    if abort and not rv:
251	        raise Abort()
252	    return rv

255	def echo_via_pager(
256	    text_or_generator: cabc.Iterable[str] | t.Callable[[], cabc.Iterable[str]] | str,
257	    color: bool | None = None,
258	) -> None:
259	    """This function takes a text and shows it via an environment specific
260	    pager on stdout.
261	
262	    .. versionchanged:: 3.0
263	       Added the `color` flag.
264	
265	    :param text_or_generator: the text to page, or alternatively, a
266	                              generator emitting the text to page.
267	    :param color: controls if the pager supports ANSI colors or not.  The
268	                  default is autodetection.
269	    """
270	    color = resolve_color_default(color)
271	
272	    if inspect.isgeneratorfunction(text_or_generator):
273	        i = t.cast("t.Callable[[], cabc.Iterable[str]]", text_or_generator)()
274	    elif isinstance(text_or_generator, str):
275	        i = [text_or_generator]
276	    else:
277	        i = iter(t.cast("cabc.Iterable[str]", text_or_generator))
278	
279	    # convert every element of i to a text type if necessary
280	    text_generator = (el if isinstance(el, str) else str(el) for el in i)
281	
282	    from ._termui_impl import pager
283	
284	    return pager(itertools.chain(text_generator, "\n"), color)

287	def progressbar(
288	    iterable: cabc.Iterable[V] | None = None,
289	    length: int | None = None,
290	    label: str | None = None,
291	    hidden: bool = False,
292	    show_eta: bool = True,
293	    show_percent: bool | None = None,
294	    show_pos: bool = False,
295	    item_show_func: t.Callable[[V | None], str | None] | None = None,
296	    fill_char: str = "#",
297	    empty_char: str = "-",
298	    bar_template: str = "%(label)s  [%(bar)s]  %(info)s",
299	    info_sep: str = "  ",
300	    width: int = 36,
301	    file: t.TextIO | None = None,
302	    color: bool | None = None,
303	    update_min_steps: int = 1,
304	) -> ProgressBar[V]:
305	    """This function creates an iterable context manager that can be used
306	    to iterate over something while showing a progress bar.  It will
307	    either iterate over the `iterable` or `length` items (that are counted
308	    up).  While iteration happens, this function will print a rendered
309	    progress bar to the given `file` (defaults to stdout) and will attempt
310	    to calculate remaining time and more.  By default, this progress bar
311	    will not be rendered if the file is not a terminal.
312	
313	    The context manager creates the progress bar.  When the context
314	    manager is entered the progress bar is already created.  With every
315	    iteration over the progress bar, the iterable passed to the bar is
316	    advanced and the bar is updated.  When the context manager exits,
317	    a newline is printed and the progress bar is finalized on screen.
318	
319	    Note: The progress bar is currently designed for use cases where the
320	    total progress can be expected to take at least several seconds.
321	    Because of this, the ProgressBar class object won't display
322	    progress that is considered too fast, and progress where the time
323	    between steps is less than a second.
324	
325	    No printing must happen or the progress bar will be unintentionally
326	    destroyed.
327	
328	    Example usage::
329	
330	        with progressbar(items) as bar:
331	            for item in bar:
332	                do_something_with(item)
333	
334	    Alternatively, if no iterable is specified, one can manually update the
335	    progress bar through the `update()` method instead of directly
336	    iterating over the progress bar.  The update method accepts the number
337	    of steps to increment the bar with::
338	
339	        with progressbar(length=chunks.total_bytes) as bar:
340	            for chunk in chunks:
341	                process_chunk(chunk)
342	                bar.update(chunks.bytes)
343	
344	    The ``update()`` method also takes an optional value specifying the
345	    ``current_item`` at the new position. This is useful when used
346	    together with ``item_show_func`` to customize the output for each
347	    manual step::
348	
349	        with click.progressbar(
350	            length=total_size,
351	            label='Unzipping archive',
352	            item_show_func=lambda a: a.filename
353	        ) as bar:
354	            for archive in zip_file:
355	                archive.extract()
356	                bar.update(archive.size, archive)
357	
358	    :param iterable: an iterable to iterate over.  If not provided the length
359	                     is required.
360	    :param length: the number of items to iterate over.  By default the
361	                   progressbar will attempt to ask the iterator about its
362	                   length, which might or might not work.  If an iterable is
363	                   also provided this parameter can be used to override the
364	                   length.  If an iterable is not provided the progress bar
365	                   will iterate over a range of that length.
366	    :param label: the label to show next to the progress bar.
367	    :param hidden: hide the progressbar. Defaults to ``False``. When no tty is
368	        detected, it will only print the progressbar label. Setting this to
369	        ``False`` also disables that.
370	    :param show_eta: enables or disables the estimated time display.  This is
371	                     automatically disabled if the length cannot be
372	                     determined.
373	    :param show_percent: enables or disables the percentage display.  The
374	                         default is `True` if the iterable has a length or
375	                         `False` if not.
376	    :param show_pos: enables or disables the absolute position display.  The
377	                     default is `False`.
378	    :param item_show_func: A function called with the current item which
379	        can return a string to show next to the progress bar. If the
380	        function returns ``None`` nothing is shown. The current item can
381	        be ``None``, such as when entering and exiting the bar.
382	    :param fill_char: the character to use to show the filled part of the
383	                      progress bar.
384	    :param empty_char: the character to use to show the non-filled part of
385	                       the progress bar.
386	    :param bar_template: the format string to use as template for the bar.
387	                         The parameters in it are ``label`` for the label,
388	                         ``bar`` for the progress bar and ``info`` for the
389	                         info section.
390	    :param info_sep: the separator between multiple info items (eta etc.)
391	    :param width: the width of the progress bar in characters, 0 means full
392	                  terminal width
393	    :param file: The file to write to. If this is not a terminal then
394	        only the label is printed.
395	    :param color: controls if the terminal supports ANSI colors or not.  The
396	                  default is autodetection.  This is only needed if ANSI
397	                  codes are included anywhere in the progress bar output
398	                  which is not the case by default.
399	    :param update_min_steps: Render only when this many updates have
400	        completed. This allows tuning for very fast iterators.
401	
402	    .. versionadded:: 8.2
403	        The ``hidden`` argument.
404	
405	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
406	        Output is shown even if execution time is less than 0.5 seconds.
407	
408	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
409	        ``item_show_func`` shows the current item, not the previous one.
410	
411	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
412	        Labels are echoed if the output is not a TTY. Reverts a change
413	        in 7.0 that removed all output.
414	
415	    .. versionadded:: 8.0
416	       The ``update_min_steps`` parameter.
417	
418	    .. versionadded:: 4.0
419	        The ``color`` parameter and ``update`` method.
420	
421	    .. versionadded:: 2.0
422	    """
423	    from ._termui_impl import ProgressBar
424	
425	    color = resolve_color_default(color)
426	    return ProgressBar(
427	        iterable=iterable,
428	        length=length,
429	        hidden=hidden,
430	        show_eta=show_eta,
431	        show_percent=show_percent,
432	        show_pos=show_pos,
433	        item_show_func=item_show_func,
434	        fill_char=fill_char,
435	        empty_char=empty_char,
436	        bar_template=bar_template,
437	        info_sep=info_sep,
438	        file=file,
439	        label=label,
440	        width=width,
441	        color=color,
442	        update_min_steps=update_min_steps,
443	    )

513	    * ``bright_cyan``
514	    * ``bright_white``
515	    * ``reset`` (reset the color code only)
516	
517	    If the terminal supports it, color may also be specified as:
518	
519	    -   An integer in the interval [0, 255]. The terminal must support
520	        8-bit/256-color mode.
521	    -   An RGB tuple of three integers in [0, 255]. The terminal must
522	        support 24-bit/true-color mode.
523	
524	    See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_color and
525	    https://gist.github.com/XVilka/8346728 for more information.
526	
527	    :param text: the string to style with ansi codes.
528	    :param fg: if provided this will become the foreground color.
529	    :param bg: if provided this will become the background color.
530	    :param bold: if provided this will enable or disable bold mode.
531	    :param dim: if provided this will enable or disable dim mode.  This is
532	                badly supported.
533	    :param underline: if provided this will enable or disable underline.
534	    :param overline: if provided this will enable or disable overline.
535	    :param italic: if provided this will enable or disable italic.
536	    :param blink: if provided this will enable or disable blinking.
537	    :param reverse: if provided this will enable or disable inverse
538	                    rendering (foreground becomes background and the
539	                    other way round).
540	    :param strikethrough: if provided this will enable or disable
541	        striking through text.
542	    :param reset: by default a reset-all code is added at the end of the
543	                  string which means that styles do not carry over.  This
544	                  can be disabled to compose styles.
545	
546	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
547	        A non-string ``message`` is converted to a string.
548	
549	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
550	       Added support for 256 and RGB color codes.
551	
552	    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
553	        Added the ``strikethrough``, ``italic``, and ``overline``
554	        parameters.
555	
556	    .. versionchanged:: 7.0
557	        Added support for bright colors.
558	
559	    .. versionadded:: 2.0
560	    """
561	    if not isinstance(text, str):
562	        text = str(text)
563	
564	    bits = []
565	
566	    if fg:
567	        try:
568	            bits.append(f"\033[{_interpret_color(fg)}m")
569	        except KeyError:
570	            raise TypeError(f"Unknown color {fg!r}") from None
571	
572	    if bg:
573	        try:
574	            bits.append(f"\033[{_interpret_color(bg, 10)}m")
575	        except KeyError:
576	            raise TypeError(f"Unknown color {bg!r}") from None
577	
578	    if bold is not None:
579	        bits.append(f"\033[{1 if bold else 22}m")
580	    if dim is not None:
581	        bits.append(f"\033[{2 if dim else 22}m")
582	    if underline is not None:
583	        bits.append(f"\033[{4 if underline else 24}m")
584	    if overline is not None:
585	        bits.append(f"\033[{53 if overline else 55}m")
586	    if italic is not None:
587	        bits.append(f"\033[{3 if italic else 23}m")
588	    if blink is not None:
589	        bits.append(f"\033[{5 if blink else 25}m")
590	    if reverse is not None:
591	        bits.append(f"\033[{7 if reverse else 27}m")
592	    if strikethrough is not None:
593	        bits.append(f"\033[{9 if strikethrough else 29}m")
594	    bits.append(text)
595	    if reset:
596	        bits.append(_ansi_reset_all)
597	    return "".join(bits)

614	    file: t.IO[t.AnyStr] | None = None,
615	    nl: bool = True,

621	    call.  As such the following two calls are the same::
622	
623	        click.secho('Hello World!', fg='green')
624	        click.echo(click.style('Hello World!', fg='green'))

627	    depending on which one they go with.
628	
629	    Non-string types will be converted to :class:`str`. However,
630	    :class:`bytes` are passed directly to :meth:`echo` without applying
631	    style. If you want to style bytes that represent text, call
632	    :meth:`bytes.decode` first.

636	        passed through without style applied.

638	    .. versionadded:: 2.0
639	    """

647	    text: t.AnyStr | None = None,

656	    system search path is used for finding the executable) it overrides
657	    the detected editor.  Optionally, some environment variables can be
658	    used.  If the editor is closed without changes, `None` is returned.  In
659	    case a file is edited directly the return value is always `None` and
660	    `require_save` and `extension` are ignored.

663

665	    automatically converted from POSIX to Windows and vice versa.  As such,
666	    the message here will have ``\n`` as newline markers.
667	
668	    :param text: the text to edit.
669	    :param editor: optionally the editor to use.  Defaults to automatic
670	                   detection.
671	    :param env: environment variables to forward to the editor.
672	    :param require_save: if this is true, then not saving in the editor
673	                         will make the return value become `None`.
674	    :param extension: the extension to tell the editor about.  This defaults
675	                      to `.txt` but changing this might change syntax
676	                      highlighting.

678	                     provided text contents.  It will not use a temporary
679	                     file as an indirection in that case.

763	    """This command stops execution and waits for the user to press any
764	    key to continue.  This is similar to the Windows batch "pause"
765	    command.  If the program is not run through a terminal, this command
766	    will instead do nothing.

from __future__ import annotations

import collections.abc as cabc
import inspect
import io
import itertools
import sys
import typing as t
from contextlib import AbstractContextManager
from gettext import gettext as _

from ._compat import isatty
from ._compat import strip_ansi
from .exceptions import Abort
from .exceptions import UsageError
from .globals import resolve_color_default
from .types import Choice
from .types import convert_type
from .types import ParamType
from .utils import echo
from .utils import LazyFile

if t.TYPE_CHECKING:
    from ._termui_impl import ProgressBar

V = t.TypeVar("V")

# The prompt functions to use.  The doc tools currently override these
# functions to customize how they work.
visible_prompt_func: t.Callable[[str], str] = input

_ansi_colors = {
    "black": 30,
    "red": 31,
    "green": 32,
    "yellow": 33,
    "blue": 34,
    "magenta": 35,
    "cyan": 36,
    "white": 37,
    "reset": 39,
    "bright_black": 90,
    "bright_red": 91,
    "bright_green": 92,
    "bright_yellow": 93,
    "bright_blue": 94,
    "bright_magenta": 95,
    "bright_cyan": 96,
    "bright_white": 97,
}
_ansi_reset_all = "\033[0m"


def hidden_prompt_func(prompt: str) -> str:
    import getpass

    return getpass.getpass(prompt)


def _build_prompt(
    text: str,
    suffix: str,
    show_default: bool = False,
    default: t.Any | None = None,
    show_choices: bool = True,
    type: ParamType | None = None,
) -> str:
    prompt = text
    if type is not None and show_choices and isinstance(type, Choice):
        prompt += f" ({', '.join(map(str, type.choices))})"
    if default is not None and show_default:
        prompt = f"{prompt} [{_format_default(default)}]"
    return f"{prompt}{suffix}"


def _format_default(default: t.Any) -> t.Any:
    if isinstance(default, (io.IOBase, LazyFile)) and hasattr(default, "name"):
        return default.name

    return default


def prompt(
    text: str,
    default: t.Any | None = None,
    hide_input: bool = False,
    confirmation_prompt: bool | str = False,
    type: ParamType | t.Any | None = None,
    value_proc: t.Callable[[str], t.Any] | None = None,
    prompt_suffix: str = ": ",
    show_default: bool = True,
    err: bool = False,
    show_choices: bool = True,
) -> t.Any:
    """Prompts a user for input.  This is a convenience function that can
    be used to prompt a user for input later.

    If the user aborts the input by sending an interrupt signal, this
    function will catch it and raise a :exc:`Abort` exception.

    :param text: the text to show for the prompt.
    :param default: the default value to use if no input happens.  If this
                    is not given it will prompt until it's aborted.
    :param hide_input: if this is set to true then the input value will
                       be hidden.
    :param confirmation_prompt: Prompt a second time to confirm the
        value. Can be set to a string instead of ``True`` to customize
        the message.
    :param type: the type to use to check the value against.
    :param value_proc: if this parameter is provided it's a function that
                       is invoked instead of the type conversion to
                       convert a value.
    :param prompt_suffix: a suffix that should be added to the prompt.
    :param show_default: shows or hides the default value in the prompt.
    :param err: if set to true the file defaults to ``stderr`` instead of
                ``stdout``, the same as with echo.
    :param show_choices: Show or hide choices if the passed type is a Choice.
                         For example if type is a Choice of either day or week,
                         show_choices is true and text is "Group by" then the
                         prompt will be "Group by (day, week): ".

    .. versionadded:: 8.0
        ``confirmation_prompt`` can be a custom string.

    .. versionadded:: 7.0
        Added the ``show_choices`` parameter.

    .. versionadded:: 6.0
        Added unicode support for cmd.exe on Windows.

    .. versionadded:: 4.0
        Added the `err` parameter.

    """

    def prompt_func(text: str) -> str:
        f = hidden_prompt_func if hide_input else visible_prompt_func
        try:
            # Write the prompt separately so that we get nice
            # coloring through colorama on Windows
            echo(text.rstrip(" "), nl=False, err=err)
            # Echo a space to stdout to work around an issue where
            # readline causes backspace to clear the whole line.
            return f(" ")
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, EOFError):
            # getpass doesn't print a newline if the user aborts input with ^C.
            # Allegedly this behavior is inherited from getpass(3).
            # A doc bug has been filed at https://bugs.python.org/issue24711
            if hide_input:
                echo(None, err=err)
            raise Abort() from None

    if value_proc is None:
        value_proc = convert_type(type, default)

    prompt = _build_prompt(
        text, prompt_suffix, show_default, default, show_choices, type
    )

    if confirmation_prompt:
        if confirmation_prompt is True:
            confirmation_prompt = _("Repeat for confirmation")

        confirmation_prompt = _build_prompt(confirmation_prompt, prompt_suffix)

    while True:
        while True:
            value = prompt_func(prompt)
            if value:
                break
            elif default is not None:
                value = default
                break
        try:
            result = value_proc(value)
        except UsageError as e:
            if hide_input:
                echo(_("Error: The value you entered was invalid."), err=err)
            else:
                echo(_("Error: {e.message}").format(e=e), err=err)
            continue
        if not confirmation_prompt:
            return result
        while True:
            value2 = prompt_func(confirmation_prompt)
            is_empty = not value and not value2
            if value2 or is_empty:
                break
        if value == value2:
            return result
        echo(_("Error: The two entered values do not match."), err=err)


def confirm(
    text: str,
    default: bool | None = False,
    abort: bool = False,
    prompt_suffix: str = ": ",
    show_default: bool = True,
    err: bool = False,
) -> bool:
    """Prompts for confirmation (yes/no question).

    If the user aborts the input by sending a interrupt signal this
    function will catch it and raise a :exc:`Abort` exception.

    :param text: the question to ask.
    :param default: The default value to use when no input is given. If
        ``None``, repeat until input is given.
    :param abort: if this is set to `True` a negative answer aborts the
                  exception by raising :exc:`Abort`.
    :param prompt_suffix: a suffix that should be added to the prompt.
    :param show_default: shows or hides the default value in the prompt.
    :param err: if set to true the file defaults to ``stderr`` instead of
                ``stdout``, the same as with echo.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        Repeat until input is given if ``default`` is ``None``.

    .. versionadded:: 4.0
        Added the ``err`` parameter.
    """
    prompt = _build_prompt(
        text,
        prompt_suffix,
        show_default,
        "y/n" if default is None else ("Y/n" if default else "y/N"),
    )

    while True:
        try:
            # Write the prompt separately so that we get nice
            # coloring through colorama on Windows
            echo(prompt.rstrip(" "), nl=False, err=err)
            # Echo a space to stdout to work around an issue where
            # readline causes backspace to clear the whole line.
            value = visible_prompt_func(" ").lower().strip()
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, EOFError):
            raise Abort() from None
        if value in ("y", "yes"):
            rv = True
        elif value in ("n", "no"):
            rv = False
        elif default is not None and value == "":
            rv = default
        else:
            echo(_("Error: invalid input"), err=err)
            continue
        break
    if abort and not rv:
        raise Abort()
    return rv


def echo_via_pager(
    text_or_generator: cabc.Iterable[str] | t.Callable[[], cabc.Iterable[str]] | str,
    color: bool | None = None,
) -> None:
    """This function takes a text and shows it via an environment specific
    pager on stdout.

    .. versionchanged:: 3.0
       Added the `color` flag.

    :param text_or_generator: the text to page, or alternatively, a
                              generator emitting the text to page.
    :param color: controls if the pager supports ANSI colors or not.  The
                  default is autodetection.
    """
    color = resolve_color_default(color)

    if inspect.isgeneratorfunction(text_or_generator):
        i = t.cast("t.Callable[[], cabc.Iterable[str]]", text_or_generator)()
    elif isinstance(text_or_generator, str):
        i = [text_or_generator]
    else:
        i = iter(t.cast("cabc.Iterable[str]", text_or_generator))

    # convert every element of i to a text type if necessary
    text_generator = (el if isinstance(el, str) else str(el) for el in i)

    from ._termui_impl import pager

    return pager(itertools.chain(text_generator, "\n"), color)


def progressbar(
    iterable: cabc.Iterable[V] | None = None,
    length: int | None = None,
    label: str | None = None,
    hidden: bool = False,
    show_eta: bool = True,
    show_percent: bool | None = None,
    show_pos: bool = False,
    item_show_func: t.Callable[[V | None], str | None] | None = None,
    fill_char: str = "#",
    empty_char: str = "-",
    bar_template: str = "%(label)s  [%(bar)s]  %(info)s",
    info_sep: str = "  ",
    width: int = 36,
    file: t.TextIO | None = None,
    color: bool | None = None,
    update_min_steps: int = 1,
) -> ProgressBar[V]:
    """This function creates an iterable context manager that can be used
    to iterate over something while showing a progress bar.  It will
    either iterate over the `iterable` or `length` items (that are counted
    up).  While iteration happens, this function will print a rendered
    progress bar to the given `file` (defaults to stdout) and will attempt
    to calculate remaining time and more.  By default, this progress bar
    will not be rendered if the file is not a terminal.

    The context manager creates the progress bar.  When the context
    manager is entered the progress bar is already created.  With every
    iteration over the progress bar, the iterable passed to the bar is
    advanced and the bar is updated.  When the context manager exits,
    a newline is printed and the progress bar is finalized on screen.

    Note: The progress bar is currently designed for use cases where the
    total progress can be expected to take at least several seconds.
    Because of this, the ProgressBar class object won't display
    progress that is considered too fast, and progress where the time
    between steps is less than a second.

    No printing must happen or the progress bar will be unintentionally
    destroyed.

    Example usage::

        with progressbar(items) as bar:
            for item in bar:
                do_something_with(item)

    Alternatively, if no iterable is specified, one can manually update the
    progress bar through the `update()` method instead of directly
    iterating over the progress bar.  The update method accepts the number
    of steps to increment the bar with::

        with progressbar(length=chunks.total_bytes) as bar:
            for chunk in chunks:
                process_chunk(chunk)
                bar.update(chunks.bytes)

    The ``update()`` method also takes an optional value specifying the
    ``current_item`` at the new position. This is useful when used
    together with ``item_show_func`` to customize the output for each
    manual step::

        with click.progressbar(
            length=total_size,
            label='Unzipping archive',
            item_show_func=lambda a: a.filename
        ) as bar:
            for archive in zip_file:
                archive.extract()
                bar.update(archive.size, archive)

    :param iterable: an iterable to iterate over.  If not provided the length
                     is required.
    :param length: the number of items to iterate over.  By default the
                   progressbar will attempt to ask the iterator about its
                   length, which might or might not work.  If an iterable is
                   also provided this parameter can be used to override the
                   length.  If an iterable is not provided the progress bar
                   will iterate over a range of that length.
    :param label: the label to show next to the progress bar.
    :param hidden: hide the progressbar. Defaults to ``False``. When no tty is
        detected, it will only print the progressbar label. Setting this to
        ``False`` also disables that.
    :param show_eta: enables or disables the estimated time display.  This is
                     automatically disabled if the length cannot be
                     determined.
    :param show_percent: enables or disables the percentage display.  The
                         default is `True` if the iterable has a length or
                         `False` if not.
    :param show_pos: enables or disables the absolute position display.  The
                     default is `False`.
    :param item_show_func: A function called with the current item which
        can return a string to show next to the progress bar. If the
        function returns ``None`` nothing is shown. The current item can
        be ``None``, such as when entering and exiting the bar.
    :param fill_char: the character to use to show the filled part of the
                      progress bar.
    :param empty_char: the character to use to show the non-filled part of
                       the progress bar.
    :param bar_template: the format string to use as template for the bar.
                         The parameters in it are ``label`` for the label,
                         ``bar`` for the progress bar and ``info`` for the
                         info section.
    :param info_sep: the separator between multiple info items (eta etc.)
    :param width: the width of the progress bar in characters, 0 means full
                  terminal width
    :param file: The file to write to. If this is not a terminal then
        only the label is printed.
    :param color: controls if the terminal supports ANSI colors or not.  The
                  default is autodetection.  This is only needed if ANSI
                  codes are included anywhere in the progress bar output
                  which is not the case by default.
    :param update_min_steps: Render only when this many updates have
        completed. This allows tuning for very fast iterators.

    .. versionadded:: 8.2
        The ``hidden`` argument.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        Output is shown even if execution time is less than 0.5 seconds.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        ``item_show_func`` shows the current item, not the previous one.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        Labels are echoed if the output is not a TTY. Reverts a change
        in 7.0 that removed all output.

    .. versionadded:: 8.0
       The ``update_min_steps`` parameter.

    .. versionadded:: 4.0
        The ``color`` parameter and ``update`` method.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0
    """
    from ._termui_impl import ProgressBar

    color = resolve_color_default(color)
    return ProgressBar(
        iterable=iterable,

        empty_char=empty_char,
        bar_template=bar_template,
        info_sep=info_sep,

def clear() -> None:
    """Clears the terminal screen.  This will have the effect of clearing
    the whole visible space of the terminal and moving the cursor to the
    top left.  This does not do anything if not connected to a terminal.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0
    """
    if not isatty(sys.stdout):
        return

    # ANSI escape \033[2J clears the screen, \033[1;1H moves the cursor
    echo("\033[2J\033[1;1H", nl=False)


def _interpret_color(color: int | tuple[int, int, int] | str, offset: int = 0) -> str:
    if isinstance(color, int):
        return f"{38 + offset};5;{color:d}"

    if isinstance(color, (tuple, list)):
        r, g, b = color
        return f"{38 + offset};2;{r:d};{g:d};{b:d}"

    return str(_ansi_colors[color] + offset)


def style(
    text: t.Any,
    fg: int | tuple[int, int, int] | str | None = None,
    bg: int | tuple[int, int, int] | str | None = None,
    bold: bool | None = None,
    dim: bool | None = None,
    underline: bool | None = None,
    overline: bool | None = None,
    italic: bool | None = None,
    blink: bool | None = None,
    reverse: bool | None = None,
    strikethrough: bool | None = None,
    reset: bool = True,
) -> str:
    """Styles a text with ANSI styles and returns the new string.  By
    default the styling is self contained which means that at the end
    of the string a reset code is issued.  This can be prevented by
    passing ``reset=False``.

    Examples::

        click.echo(click.style('Hello World!', fg='green'))
        click.echo(click.style('ATTENTION!', blink=True))
        click.echo(click.style('Some things', reverse=True, fg='cyan'))
        click.echo(click.style('More colors', fg=(255, 12, 128), bg=117))

    Supported color names:

    * ``black`` (might be a gray)
    * ``red``
    * ``green``
    * ``yellow`` (might be an orange)
    * ``blue``
    * ``magenta``
    * ``cyan``
    * ``white`` (might be light gray)
    * ``bright_black``
    * ``bright_red``
    * ``bright_green``
    * ``bright_yellow``
    * ``bright_blue``
    * ``bright_magenta``
    * ``bright_cyan``
    * ``bright_white``
    * ``reset`` (reset the color code only)

    If the terminal supports it, color may also be specified as:

    -   An integer in the interval [0, 255]. The terminal must support
        8-bit/256-color mode.
    -   An RGB tuple of three integers in [0, 255]. The terminal must
        support 24-bit/true-color mode.

    See https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_color and
    https://gist.github.com/XVilka/8346728 for more information.

    :param text: the string to style with ansi codes.
    :param fg: if provided this will become the foreground color.
    :param bg: if provided this will become the background color.
    :param bold: if provided this will enable or disable bold mode.
    :param dim: if provided this will enable or disable dim mode.  This is
                badly supported.
    :param underline: if provided this will enable or disable underline.
    :param overline: if provided this will enable or disable overline.
    :param italic: if provided this will enable or disable italic.
    :param blink: if provided this will enable or disable blinking.
    :param reverse: if provided this will enable or disable inverse
                    rendering (foreground becomes background and the
                    other way round).
    :param strikethrough: if provided this will enable or disable
        striking through text.
    :param reset: by default a reset-all code is added at the end of the
                  string which means that styles do not carry over.  This
                  can be disabled to compose styles.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        A non-string ``message`` is converted to a string.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
       Added support for 256 and RGB color codes.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        Added the ``strikethrough``, ``italic``, and ``overline``
        parameters.

    .. versionchanged:: 7.0
        Added support for bright colors.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0
    """
    if not isinstance(text, str):
        text = str(text)

    bits = []

    if fg:
        try:
            bits.append(f"\033[{_interpret_color(fg)}m")
        except KeyError:
            raise TypeError(f"Unknown color {fg!r}") from None

    if bg:
        try:
            bits.append(f"\033[{_interpret_color(bg, 10)}m")
        except KeyError:
            raise TypeError(f"Unknown color {bg!r}") from None

    if bold is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{1 if bold else 22}m")
    if dim is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{2 if dim else 22}m")
    if underline is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{4 if underline else 24}m")
    if overline is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{53 if overline else 55}m")
    if italic is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{3 if italic else 23}m")
    if blink is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{5 if blink else 25}m")
    if reverse is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{7 if reverse else 27}m")
    if strikethrough is not None:
        bits.append(f"\033[{9 if strikethrough else 29}m")
    bits.append(text)
    if reset:
        bits.append(_ansi_reset_all)
    return "".join(bits)


def unstyle(text: str) -> str:
    """Removes ANSI styling information from a string.  Usually it's not
    necessary to use this function as Click's echo function will
    automatically remove styling if necessary.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0

    :param text: the text to remove style information from.
    """
    return strip_ansi(text)


def secho(
    message: t.Any | None = None,
    file: t.IO[t.AnyStr] | None = None,
    nl: bool = True,
    err: bool = False,
    color: bool | None = None,
    **styles: t.Any,
) -> None:
    """This function combines :func:`echo` and :func:`style` into one
    call.  As such the following two calls are the same::

        click.secho('Hello World!', fg='green')
        click.echo(click.style('Hello World!', fg='green'))

    All keyword arguments are forwarded to the underlying functions
    depending on which one they go with.

    Non-string types will be converted to :class:`str`. However,
    :class:`bytes` are passed directly to :meth:`echo` without applying
    style. If you want to style bytes that represent text, call
    :meth:`bytes.decode` first.

    .. versionchanged:: 8.0
        A non-string ``message`` is converted to a string. Bytes are
        passed through without style applied.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0
    """
    if message is not None and not isinstance(message, (bytes, bytearray)):
        message = style(message, **styles)

    return echo(message, file=file, nl=nl, err=err, color=color)


def edit(
    text: t.AnyStr | None = None,
    editor: str | None = None,
    env: cabc.Mapping[str, str] | None = None,
    require_save: bool = True,
    extension: str = ".txt",
    filename: str | None = None,
) -> t.AnyStr | None:
    r"""Edits the given text in the defined editor.  If an editor is given
    (should be the full path to the executable but the regular operating
    system search path is used for finding the executable) it overrides
    the detected editor.  Optionally, some environment variables can be
    used.  If the editor is closed without changes, `None` is returned.  In
    case a file is edited directly the return value is always `None` and
    `require_save` and `extension` are ignored.

    If the editor cannot be opened a :exc:`UsageError` is raised.

    Note for Windows: to simplify cross-platform usage, the newlines are
    automatically converted from POSIX to Windows and vice versa.  As such,
    the message here will have ``\n`` as newline markers.

    :param text: the text to edit.
    :param editor: optionally the editor to use.  Defaults to automatic
                   detection.
    :param env: environment variables to forward to the editor.
    :param require_save: if this is true, then not saving in the editor
                         will make the return value become `None`.
    :param extension: the extension to tell the editor about.  This defaults
                      to `.txt` but changing this might change syntax
                      highlighting.
    :param filename: if provided it will edit this file instead of the
                     provided text contents.  It will not use a temporary
                     file as an indirection in that case.
    """
    from ._termui_impl import Editor

    ed = Editor(editor=editor, env=env, require_save=require_save, extension=extension)

    if filename is None:
        return ed.edit(text)

    ed.edit_file(filename)
    return None


def launch(url: str, wait: bool = False, locate: bool = False) -> int:
    """This function launches the given URL (or filename) in the default
    viewer application for this file type.  If this is an executable, it
    might launch the executable in a new session.  The return value is
    the exit code of the launched application.  Usually, ``0`` indicates
    success.

    Examples::

        click.launch('https://click.palletsprojects.com/')
        click.launch('/my/downloaded/file', locate=True)

    .. versionadded:: 2.0

    :param url: URL or filename of the thing to launch.
    :param wait: Wait for the program to exit before returning. This
        only works if the launched program blocks. In particular,
        ``xdg-open`` on Linux does not block.
    :param locate: if this is set to `True` then instead of launching the
                   application associated with the URL it will attempt to
                   launch a file manager with the file located.  This
                   might have weird effects if the URL does not point to
                   the filesystem.
    """
    from ._termui_impl import open_url

    return open_url(url, wait=wait, locate=locate)


# If this is provided, getchar() calls into this instead.  This is used
# for unittesting purposes.
_getchar: t.Callable[[bool], str] | None = None


def getchar(echo: bool = False) -> str:
    """Fetches a single character from the terminal and returns it.  This
    will always return a unicode character and under certain rare
    circumstances this might return more than one character.  The
    situations which more than one character is returned is when for
    whatever reason multiple characters end up in the terminal buffer or
    standard input was not actually a terminal.

    Note that this will always read from the terminal, even if something
    is piped into the standard input.

    Note for Windows: in rare cases when typing non-ASCII characters, this
    function might wait for a second character and then return both at once.
    This is because certain Unicode characters look like special-key markers.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0

    :param echo: if set to `True`, the character read will also show up on
                 the terminal.  The default is to not show it.
    """
    global _getchar

    if _getchar is None:
        from ._termui_impl import getchar as f

        _getchar = f

    return _getchar(echo)


def raw_terminal() -> AbstractContextManager[int]:
    from ._termui_impl import raw_terminal as f

    return f()


def pause(info: str | None = None, err: bool = False) -> None:
    """This command stops execution and waits for the user to press any
    key to continue.  This is similar to the Windows batch "pause"
    command.  If the program is not run through a terminal, this command
    will instead do nothing.

    .. versionadded:: 2.0

    .. versionadded:: 4.0
       Added the `err` parameter.

    :param info: The message to print before pausing. Defaults to
        ``"Press any key to continue..."``.
    :param err: if set to message goes to ``stderr`` instead of
                ``stdout``, the same as with echo.
    """
    if not isatty(sys.stdin) or not isatty(sys.stdout):
        return

    if info is None:
        info = _("Press any key to continue...")

    try:
        if info:
            echo(info, nl=False, err=err)
        try:
            getchar()
        except (KeyboardInterrupt, EOFError):
            pass
    finally:
        if info:
            echo(err=err)

) -> str:
